content:
# 1 John Chapter 1 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.1 — "That which was from the beginning" vs. "the one who existed from the beginning"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What was from [the] beginning" | ho ēn ap' archēs — neuter relative pronoun |
| LOGOS | "That which was from the beginning" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the one who existed from the beginning" | Personal "the one who" for neuter ho |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek uses a neuter relative pronoun (ho, "that which"), not a masculine one (hos, "he who"). The author deliberately begins with the impersonal — "that which" — before gradually revealing the personal (v.2: "the life"; v.3: "his Son Jesus Christ"). PNEUMA's "the one who" makes it personal from the first word, collapsing the literary unfolding. The neuter conveys both the message and the person — a deliberate ambiguity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA personalizes the neuter.

---

### v.1 — "touched with our hands" vs. "touched him with our own hands"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "our hands handled" | hai cheires hēmōn epsēlaphēsan |
| LOGOS | "have touched with our hands" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "touched him with our own hands" | Adds "him" and "own" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek verb epsēlaphēsan ("handled/touched") has no direct object — it is absolute. PNEUMA adds "him," which makes the object explicit. While Jesus is clearly the referent, the author's choice to leave the verb without an object maintains the sensory emphasis: the touching itself, not just who was touched.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds explicit object.

---

### v.6 — "walk in darkness" vs. "go on living in spiritual darkness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "walk in the darkness" | en tō skotei peripatōmen |
| LOGOS | "walk in darkness" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "go on living in spiritual darkness" | "Spiritual" added; "living" for "walking" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says "walk in darkness" (peripatōmen en tō skotei) — not "spiritual darkness." The Johannine light/darkness motif is deliberately comprehensive, encompassing ethical, spiritual, and relational dimensions simultaneously. Adding "spiritual" narrows the scope. "Walk" (peripateō) is the standard NT metaphor for conduct and should be preserved.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA qualifies the darkness.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA personalizes the impersonal | 1 | "The one who" for neuter "that which" (v.1) |
| PNEUMA adds explicit objects | 1 | "Him" added to absolute verb (v.1) |
| PNEUMA qualifies/narrows | 1 | "Spiritual darkness" for "darkness" (v.6) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 1's most significant disagreement is v.1 — the neuter pronoun. The author's choice to begin with "that which" rather than "he who" is theologically deliberate: the Word of life is first encountered as reality (heard, seen, touched) before being named as a person. PNEUMA's personalizing instinct is understandable but premature.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
